steps.title: Step 1 — divide the leading terms
steps.body: Divide $x^3 - 4x^2 + x + 6$ by $(x-2)$. Divide the leading term of the polynomial by the leading term of the divisor: $x^3 \div x = x^2$. This is the first term of the quotient.

steps.title: Step 2 — multiply back and subtract
steps.body: Multiply the divisor by $x^2$: $x^2(x-2) = x^3 - 2x^2$. Subtract this from the original polynomial: $(x^3-4x^2+x+6) - (x^3-2x^2) = -2x^2 + x + 6$.

steps.title: Step 3 — repeat with the new leading term
steps.body: Divide $-2x^2 \div x = -2x$ — the next term of the quotient. Multiply back: $-2x(x-2) = -2x^2 + 4x$. Subtract: $(-2x^2+x+6) - (-2x^2+4x) = -3x + 6$.

steps.title: Step 4 — final term
steps.body: Divide $-3x \div x = -3$ — the last term of the quotient. Multiply back: $-3(x-2) = -3x+6$. Subtract: $(-3x+6)-(-3x+6) = 0$. Remainder $0$, so the division is exact.

steps.title: Step 5 — write the result
steps.body: Collecting the quotient terms $x^2 - 2x - 3$ gives $x^3-4x^2+x+6 = (x-2)(x^2-2x-3)$. **Check** by expanding: $(x-2)(x^2-2x-3) = x^3-2x^2-3x-2x^2+4x+6 = x^3-4x^2+x+6$. ✓ This matches the original polynomial exactly.
